checks.a_analysis: The output correctly captures each text block once, following the sequence of the source PDF. There is no evidence of duplicated paragraphs or sections.

The document contains one heading: '10A NCAC 22B .0102 COORDINATION WITH TITLE XVIII'. The output assigns this as a level 2 heading. Given that the document title in metadata often serves as the H1, assigning major section headings as H2 is a standard and acceptable practice for screen reader navigation.
The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no table structure corruption can occur.

The reading order in the JSON perfectly mirrors the vertical flow of the source document, starting from the introductory sentence, through the heading and main paragraph, and ending with the History Note section.

The source document contains a 'History Note' section which is formatted as a series of vertically stacked items. While not explicitly bulleted, it functions as a list. The output renders this as a single paragraph with newline characters (\n), which preserves the visual and semantic structure of the block. No items are merged or lost.

The URL 'http://uscode.house.gov/' present in the source text is correctly identified and converted into a link object in the output JSON. No unauthorized links were added, and no existing links were missed.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly maintains the visual organization of the document. While the line numbers from the PDF are omitted and the main document title (from line 3 of the PDF) has been promoted to a top-level heading in the HTML, this is a standard and expected transformation for accessible web content. The logical flow of the information remains intact, and the visual cues for edits (underlines and strikethroughs) are perfectly preserved.
The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, there are no tables to evaluate in the output.
The source PDF is a text-only legal document with no meaningful images, logos, or diagrams. No image elements were expected or found in the HTML output.

A careful comparison of the PDF content and the HTML screenshot shows no instances of duplicated body content. The document title appearing as a main heading and the body text beginning with its corresponding identifier is consistent with the source and does not constitute content duplication.
